hook: You already know how to measure angles, identify shapes, and use formulas — but so far you've mostly taken those facts on faith. This unit is where geometry transforms from a collection of rules into a system you can actually *verify and defend*. What does it really mean to *know* something is true in mathematics, and how can you convince someone else beyond any doubt?
concept: Every topic in this unit builds toward one big idea: turning observations and intuition into airtight logical arguments. This is the practice of mathematical proof — the art of moving from 'I think this is true' to 'I can guarantee this is true,' using the formal structure of *deductive reasoning*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to spot patterns in examples and form conjectures — educated guesses — while understanding why a pattern alone can never fully prove a mathematical claim.
Master writing 'if-then' statements, identifying their hypothesis and conclusion, and exploring whether flipping the statement around (the converse) stays true.
You will learn to combine a conditional and its converse into a single 'if and only if' statement, and recognize when a definition can be written as a true biconditional.
Apply the laws of logic — including the Law of Detachment and the Law of Syllogism — to draw guaranteed conclusions from given true statements.
You will learn to organize a logical argument into the two-column proof format, pairing each statement with the property, definition, or theorem that justifies it.
Apply two-column proof structure to rigorously prove real geometric relationships, such as the Segment Addition Postulate and the Angle Congruence Theorems.
Master two alternative proof formats — the flowing narrative of a paragraph proof and the visual logic chain of a flow proof — to express the same airtight reasoning in different styles.
